data.title: Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon: North Korean quintet among invited runners
data.description:
The organisers of the Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon have invited two of the world’s top 100 runners and a group of North Koreans to be part of this year’s race.
Of the 26 men and 19 women included in the elite category, Kenya and Ethiopia have sent 37 athletes to compete, while there is also an Asian Games silver medallist in the mix.
The fastest man in the field is Ethiopia’s Mekuant Ayenew, who is ranked 98th in the world and has a personal best time of 2 hours, four minutes and 46 seconds. He will wear bib No 1 on Sunday.
Bahrain’s Shitaye Eshete is the quickest woman, with a fastest time of 2:20:32, which she clocked at last year’s Haspa Marathon Hamburg.
The elite list also features one man and four women from North Korea, including Asian champion and North Korea’s national record holder Han Il-ryong.

Han, who drew attention at the 2022 Hangzhou Asian Games by winning a silver medal without using top-end carbon shoes or energy gels, ran his personal best of 2:09:42 at the 2024 Wuxi Marathon and will wear bib No 19.
Among the North Korean women, the fastest is the 22-year-old Jon Su-gyong, with a best of 2:25:48.
This is not the first time North Korean runners have raced in the city, with athletes from the secretive state enjoying a storied history at the marathon.
Kim Hye-gyong claimed the women’s title in 2:31:46 in 2015, while in 2008 Kim Kum-ok and Jong Yong-ok memorably crossed the finish line hand in hand, with the then 19-year-old Kim taking the crown.
She later finished fourth in 2012 and third in 2013 while also winning the Asian Championships.
Japan’s famed Yuki Kawauchi will also return to the city after finishing seventh in last year’s Bank of China Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge Half Marathon, in what will be his first invited appearance at the Standard Chartered-backed event.
A former civil servant, Kawauchi gained fame by winning the 2018 Boston Marathon as an amateur runner, earning the title of “The Citizen Runner”.

Kawauchi was a hit with local runners on his last Hong Kong visit, being mobbed for autographs and selfies after the race.
Taking place this Sunday, the city’s largest marathon event expects to draw 74,000 runners across the full marathon, half-marathon, 10km and wheelchair 10km races, with 25 per cent of them from mainland China or overseas – the highest ever.
Those runners come from 110 countries and regions, following a total of nearly 120,000 record sign-ups for the entry lottery.
This year’s race offers a total prize pot of US$314,800, with the men’s and women’s full marathon champions each earning US$65,000. An additional US$12,000 is on offer for any athlete who breaks the course record.

data.title: European allies send troops to Greenland amid Trump’s purchase pressure
data.description:
European countries were sending small numbers of military personnel to Greenland on Thursday as Denmark and its allies ‌prepared for exercises to try to assure US President Donald Trump of its security as he pushes to acquire the island.
A meeting of officials from the United States, Denmark ‍and Greenland on Wednesday avoided the type of public humiliation meted out to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky last year, but also produced no quick fix for the dispute.
“The American ambition to take over Greenland is intact,” Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen said in a written comment to Reuters on Thursday, describing a “fundamental disagreement”.
“That is of course serious, and therefore we continue our efforts to prevent this scenario becoming a reality.”
Trump cites Russian and Chinese interests
Trump has said the strategically located and mineral-rich island is ⁠vital to US security and that the US must own it to prevent Russia or China occupying it. He has said all options are on the table for securing the territory, which is an autonomous territory of Denmark.
He says Denmark cannot ward off Russian and Chinese influence in the Arctic region.
Russia said Nato’s talk of Moscow and Beijing being a threat to Greenland was a myth designed to whip up hysteria and warned of the dangers of escalating confrontation in the region.
There is currently little evidence that a large number of Chinese and Russian ships sail near Greenland’s coasts.
Greenland and Denmark say the island is not for sale, ‍that threats of force are reckless and security concerns should be resolved among allies.
Prominent EU countries have backed Denmark, warning a US military seizure of Greenland could in effect spell the end of Nato.
Before Wednesday’s meeting in ‍the US, Greenland and Denmark said they had begun to increase their military presence in and around Greenland in cooperation with Nato allies.
Germany, France, Sweden and Norway have said they are sending military staff to begin preparations for larger drills later this year.
“The Danish Armed Forces, together with a number of Arctic and European allies, will explore ‌in the coming weeks how an increased presence and exercise activity in the Arctic can be implemented in practice,” the Danish Ministry of Defence said.




Initial deployment appears small
Danish Foreign Minister Lars Lokke Rasmussen said on Wednesday about ‍200 US troops were stationed in Greenland, which has a population of around 57,000.
The scale of the planned European military build-up has not been made public, but initial deployments appear small.
The German Armed Forces were deploying a reconnaissance team of 13, first to Copenhagen, before heading on to Greenland with Danish personnel. Late on Wednesday, a Danish Air Force plane landed at Nuuk airport and personnel in military fatigues disembarked.
Sweden was sending three officers, and Norway two. Olivier Poivre d’Arvor, France’s ambassador to the Poles, said France was sending about 15 mountain specialists.
“A ‌first ⁠team of French military personnel is already on the ground and will be reinforced in the coming days by land, air and naval assets,” French President Emmanuel Macron said.
France and the European Union as a whole must be “unyielding in upholding territorial sovereignty”, he added.
One British officer was joining the reconnaissance group. The Netherlands said it was willing to send staff and would take a decision this week. Poland said it would not send soldiers.
The European military deployment to Greenland sends two messages to the US administration, said Marc Jacobsen, an associate professor at the Royal Danish Defence College.

“One is to deter, is to show that ‘if you ‌decide to do something militarily, we’re ready to defend Greenland’,” he said. “And the other purpose is to say: ‘Well, we take your critique seriously, we increase our presence, take care of our sovereignty, and improve surveillance over Greenland’.”
After meeting US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Vice-President J.D. Vance on Wednesday, Rasmussen and Greenland’s foreign minister, Vivian Motzfeldt, said the US and Denmark would form ‌a working group to discuss concerns regarding the island.
Greenland’s Prime Minister Jens-Frederik Nielsen reiterated on Facebook on Thursday that the island did not want to be governed by, or owned by, the United States, and that it would remain part of Denmark and the Nato alliance.
“Now is not the time for internal discussions. Now is the time for unity, calm and responsibility. I’m following the situation closely, and I stand with you to look after Greenland,” Jens-Frederik Nielsen said.

data.title: The US has sent a ‘dangerous’ message in Venezuela and China must act, legal expert warns
data.description:
China needs to legislate to protect itself against the risk of “external suppression” and safeguard its technology and supply chains, a legal expert with a top Beijing think tank has warned.
Li Honglei, head of a Communist Party committee that oversees two law institutes under the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences, said tighter national security legislation was needed to make the country more “resilient”.
He said the risks had been laid bare by America’s abduction of the former Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro and his wife on January 3.
That US military operation sent a “dangerous” message that Washington could increasingly try to bypass international law and enforce its own legal system on other countries, according to Li.
He made the remarks on Wednesday during a webinar on the US national security law framework held by Renmin University’s National Academy of Development and Strategy in Beijing.


Li also said the US National Security Strategy released in December – which highlights economic security – showed that Washington was moving from an “expansive” hegemony to a “defensive” position.
He said the US posture was also a reminder for Beijing that “resources must be concentrated in the fundamental areas that are crucial to our national destiny”.
Li said the country should avoid “overspending” strategically across a wide range of fields.
“[China’s] legal policies should fully safeguard technological self-reliance and supply chain security,” he said.
Li called for schemes to support basic research, core technologies and strategic material reserves that involved a solid legal grounding as well as long-term funding.
“It is not just about investment,” he said.
“More critically, it is about refining the legal system to create an institutional environment that encourages original innovation, protects intellectual property and streamlines industrial circulation.
“That is the bedrock for us to cope with all external suppression.”
It comes amid an intensifying strategic rivalry between China and the United States as the two powers vie for dominance in hi-tech sectors such as semiconductors and artificial intelligence.
Both countries are jostling for more control over the critical minerals and supply chains that power those advanced technologies.
Beijing has put more emphasis on hi-tech self-reliance and home-grown innovation, which was listed as a top priority in the proposed five-year plan to 2030 approved by the ruling Communist Party in October.
Washington, meanwhile, vowed in its latest national security document to “ensure that US technology and US standards – particularly in AI, biotech and quantum computing – drive the world forward” and to “control our own supply chains”.
At a gathering of finance ministers in Washington on Monday, Group of Seven countries “expressed a strong, shared desire to quickly address key vulnerabilities in critical minerals supply chains”, according to the US Department of the Treasury.
US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent showed “his optimism that nations will pursue prudent de-risking over decoupling” in the meeting, alluding to diversifying away from Chinese supplies.
Meanwhile, Greenland’s rich mineral wealth has been widely seen as one of the factors behind US President Donald Trump’s talk of taking over the semi-autonomous territory of Denmark, a Nato ally of the US.


Speaking during the webinar, Li said China should avoid falling into a “securitisation trap” that could hinder technological progress.
“Our national legislators and decision-makers need to establish finer risk assessment and scrutiny mechanisms to ensure that security measures are necessary, reasonable and proportionate,” he said.
He also called for the implementation rules of China’s Anti-Foreign Sanctions Law to be improved, and for tougher legislation in areas such as data and finance.
“These tools should ... create effective deterrence without undermining international cooperation networks,” Li said.
Addressing the same event, Yawei Liu, a senior adviser on China at The Carter Center, a non-profit organisation in Atlanta, noted that security was a pressing issue for both Beijing and Washington.
“Both countries are so large – economically, militarily and in terms of influence – that an action taken by either one that ignores the concerns of the other will create insecurity,” he said, adding that more face-to-face meetings between Chinese and US officials to discuss the issue were needed.

data.title: US seizes another Venezuela-linked tanker ahead of Trump-Machado meeting
data.description:
The United ‌States has seized another ‍Venezuela-linked tanker, two US officials told Reuters on Thursday, ⁠ahead of a meeting between US President Donald Trump and ‍Venezuelan opposition leader Maria Corina Machado.
The ‍seizure marks the sixth vessel ‌targeted in recent weeks that was ‍either carrying Venezuelan oil or had done so ‌in the past.
The ⁠officials, speaking on the condition of anonymity, said the seizure took ‌place in the Caribbean, but did ‌not ‌identify the vessel.
More to follow …

data.title: 3 new Hong Kong lawmakers fail to declare company shareholdings
data.description:
At least three newcomers to Hong Kong’s legislature, including two from the city’s largest political party, have failed to declare company shareholdings, the Post has found, with the trio blaming the omissions on heavy workloads or misunderstandings.
The three lawmakers are Jody Kwok Fu-yung and Hung Kam-in of the Democratic Alliance for the Betterment and Progress of Hong Kong (DAB), and Thomas So Shiu-tsung, a lawyer.
“I am still a newcomer and have to handle many matters, such as [a new] office and meetings,” Kwok told the Post on Thursday, a day after the deadline for all lawmakers to declare their interests had passed.
“I have been very cautious about my conduct, declarations of interests and speeches. This time is an honest mistake.”
Lawmakers must declare shareholdings in any companies or bodies exceeding 1 per cent of the total shares by the first Legislative Council meeting of each term, under Legco’s rules of procedure.

A Post check of the declarations of Legco’s 40 first-time lawmakers, cross-referenced with the Companies Registry, revealed that the three had failed to declare their shareholdings by Wednesday.
Kwok, who was elected in the New Territories South West geographical constituency, submitted the registration form to Legco last Friday, declaring no beneficial interests in shares of any companies or bodies.
She did not declare her shares in Light Investment (Hong Kong) Limited and Youth Vision HK Limited, in which she held 50 and 30 per cent of the shareholdings respectively, as indicated in company registry records.
Kwok also owns two plots of agricultural land in the New Territories through Light Investment (Hong Kong).
Fellow DAB lawmaker Hung, who represents the Election Committee constituency in the 90-member Legco, failed to declare his 35 per cent shareholdings in EGI Creative Limited, where he was also a director.
Hung said the company aimed to help young people start their own businesses, but it had ceased operations, leading him to believe that it had been dissolved.
“My colleague handled the dissolution matters, and it was a few years ago,” Hung said, adding that he would declare the shareholdings in due course.
Hung is also the shareholder of Hong Kong Big Band International Group Limited, a trading company, according to his declaration.

Former Law Society president Thomas So, of the Election Committee constituency, did not disclose that he was the sole shareholder of Best Full Technology Limited.
So said the company was no longer in operation and apologised for the omission.
“My team is submitting the supplementary declaration. I will strengthen our internal monitoring to ensure full compliance with declaration requirements,” So said.
His registration form, submitted on December 30, listed only his shareholdings in Concept Development Limited, a firm engaged in building technology advisory services.
A code for Legco members that took effect this term stipulates lawmakers’ registrable interests, including shareholdings. A supervisory committee will monitor legislators’ performance and compliance, and conduct investigations into complaints.




Asked how the supervisory panel would handle omissions of declarations of interest, Ronick Chan Chun-ying, chairman of Legco’s House Committee, said it had not yet been officially formed and he was unsure of its procedures.
But Chan urged the lawmakers involved to make up for it swiftly and called for public understanding.
“To declare a day before or a day after [the deadline], if it was due to being newcomers, I think the public will understand,” Chan said, stressing that some veteran members were also confused by the amended rules.
Kwok and Hung submitted supplementary declarations of their shareholdings on Thursday afternoon, following the Post’s queries.
Ex-legislator Miriam Lau Kin-yee, a former chairwoman of the House Committee, said the cases were a clear violation of the declaration-of-interest regulation, even though the company was dormant.

data.title: China warns against the ‘law of the jungle’ amid anti-government protests in Iran
data.description:
China has warned that the “law of the jungle” must not apply in Iran amid the current wave of anti-government protests.
Foreign Minister Wang Yi spoke to his Iranian counterpart Abbas Araghchi by phone on Thursday, telling him that Beijing was willing to play a “constructive role” in resolving differences through dialogue.
Some observers have described the recent protests in Iran as the largest since the 1979 Islamic revolution with an estimated death toll ranging from 2,000 to 12,000.
Araghchi told Wang the demonstrations had been “incited by external forces”, according to the Chinese foreign ministry, but said the situation had now stabilised and that Tehran “has prepared countermeasures against foreign interference”.
“The door to dialogue remains open,” he added, and said he hoped China would play a greater role in maintaining regional peace.
Wang expressed confidence in the Iranian government’s ability to overcome its difficulties and maintain national stability, the statement from Beijing added.
Wang said: “China opposes the use or threat of force in international relations, opposes imposing one’s will on other countries and opposes any regression of the world to the ‘law of the jungle’.”
US President Donald Trump has threatened military action against the Iranian authorities if the killing of protesters continued.
On Tuesday, he wrote on social media that Tehran would “pay a big price” for the killing of civilians and urged people in the country to “keep protesting”, adding that “help is on its way”.
The threats follow last week’s abduction of the former Venezuelan president Nicolas Maduro by US special forces.
However, Trump appeared to temper his rhetoric on Wednesday, saying the “killing in Iran is stopping”.
The US has redeployed an aircraft carrier strike group from the South China Sea to the Middle East, cable news network NewsNation reported on Thursday.


The USS Abraham Lincoln left its home port of San Diego in November and has been in the South China Sea for the past month, taking part in live-fire exercises near the disputed Scarborough Shoal.
According to the US Naval Institute’s tracker, there were no American aircraft carriers deployed in the Middle East as of Monday, but the Abraham Lincoln was the closest to the region of all the carrier strike groups on active duty.
Tehran has warned that it would target US forces in the Middle East if Washington launched an attack against the country, prompting a number of US and British military personnel to leave the Al-Udeid airbase in Qatar.

data.title: Chinese firms to incur higher costs for deploying advanced AI chips from US: analysts
data.description:
Chinese companies are expected to shoulder higher costs for deploying advanced chips from the US, owing to a new 25 per cent tariff imposed by Washington on select high-performance semiconductors, according to analysts.
The administration of US President Donald Trump on Wednesday said the new import duties on advanced artificial intelligence chips covered Nvidia’s H200 and Advanced Micro Devices’ MI325X graphics processing units, as well as their equivalent products and derivatives.
According to the White House directive, US reliance on foreign semiconductor supply chains posed a national security and economic risk.
The new tariff appeared to encourage increased domestic chip production and reduce US dependence on foreign manufacturing hubs such as Taiwan. This implied that chips made by contract foundries, such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co, could not be shipped directly to mainland China, but must first be routed through the US.
The directive was announced a day after the Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS), under the US Department of Commerce, gave a conditional approval for Nvidia to sell its H200 chips in China. The US required every H200 shipment to undergo independent, third-party testing in the US to verify performance specifications before export.
“Essentially, we could be seeing the long-discussed 25 per cent US government ‘cut’ on H200 and MI325X revenue being put into practice,” said Chim Lee, a senior analyst at the Economist Intelligence Unit (EIU).
Trump previously said he would allow the sale of Nvidia’s advanced chips to China in exchange for a 25 per cent cut of sales.
“This could be a legal pathway to get around the fact that the US Constitution prohibits export taxes,” Lee said.

The tariff was introduced under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, which authorised the president to levy duties on specific products.
It underscored how the US remained heavily reliant on foreign chip manufacturing supply chains, as the country consumed roughly a quarter of the world’s semiconductors, but made only a 10th of the chips it required.
The new tariff, however, would not apply to chips imported to “support the buildout of the US technology supply chain and the strengthening of domestic manufacturing capacity”.
US Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick was given broad discretion to apply further exemptions, according to the directive. The White House’s fact sheet indicated broader duties on chips and related products could follow.
According to the BIS, H200 shipments to China would be capped at no more than 50 per cent of the volume shipped to US customers, and Chinese demand would not be allowed to divert global foundry capacity from US users.
Lennart Heim, an AI policy and semiconductor expert, wrote in a post on X: “Neat trick buried in the H200 rule: every shipment needs ‘third-party testing’ in the US before export. Why?”
“This tariff then applies 25 per cent to chips matching narrow specs that go in and out again because of security risks,” Heim said. He speculated that in the future there could be 90 days of trade negotiations and broader tariffs on semiconductors under phase two of this US programme.
Meanwhile, China’s perspective on Nvidia’s H200 remained uncertain, as the country sought to establish a balance between tech self-reliance and support for AI development.
“Beijing has made it politically unpalatable for private firms to rely too much on imported AI chips,” EIU’s Lee said. “While policies from DC and Beijing will drive the narrative, we shouldn’t overlook how adaptable Chinese developers are.”
Lee said China’s technology sector was now used to working under various constraints, citing as an example DeepSeek’s breakthrough release of powerful, cost-efficient AI models last year.
Beijing was now drafting rules on how many advanced AI chips Chinese companies would be allowed to buy from foreign vendors like Nvidia, according to a Nikkei Asia report on Thursday.

data.title: Hong Kong employee insurance costs to jump 15% amid surge in day procedures: study
data.description:
More Hong Kong employees are seeking to have minor day procedures covered under the inpatient services of their group medical insurance, a joint study has found, while projecting a near 15 per cent increase in employers’ premiums this year.
The findings, published in the Hong Kong Employee Medical Insurance Index, were released on Thursday by the Hong Kong Polytechnic University in collaboration with corporate employee benefits and MPF consultancy GUM.
The index, which tracks data from Hong Kong’s group medical insurance market between 2006 and the second quarter of 2025, showed that minor day procedures such as wart removal, toenail treatments and endoscopy have become increasingly popular.
“[The minor day procedures] could apportion the risk pool originally set up for serious illness such as cancer,” GUM CEO Gloria Siu warned, adding that this would in turn drive up the cost of serious illness cover and consequently insurance premiums.
According to the report, use of inpatient plans – which cover minor day procedures and surgeries requiring hospitalisation – rose sharply, with the utilisation index climbing 13.9 per cent from 273 in 2024 to 311 in the second quarter of 2025.
The inpatient plan utilisation index measures the number of procedures per insured person, using 2006 levels as a reference point.
However, rather than advanced operations, minor day procedures accounted for an overwhelming 82 per cent of inpatient cases in the second quarter of 2025.
In particular, the average number of claims per 100 insured people for viral wart procedures increased from 5.02 in 2024 to 5.69 in the first half of 2025 – a jump of 13 per cent. Surgeries involving endoscopy also rose by 7.1 per cent, from 3.66 to 3.92.
Ingrown toenail treatments are now also among the top ten inpatient diagnoses among employees covered by group medical insurance.
As a result of rising claims for minor day procedures, the inpatient plan premium index increased by 6.3 per cent, from 411 in 2024 to 437 in the second quarter of 2025.

In contrast, demand for outpatient services has become more stabilised following rapid growth during the Covid-19 pandemic, increasing only slightly from 131 in 2024 to 134.
Experts involved in the study warned that if utilisation and cost trends continue at current levels through the year, combined with medical inflation running at around 9.73 per cent, premiums could become unaffordable for many employers.
According to their projection, the overall average premium per insured person is expected to reach HK$11,078 in 2026 – a near 15 per cent rise from HK$9,634 in the second quarter of last year.
“This will definitely be a high figure … this cost is definitely not easy [for employers] to keep up with,” Siu said.
She urged employers, insurers and the public to work together to ensure the long-term sustainability of employee medical insurance.
Human resources and planning specialist Lawrence Hung said employers should consult insurers on the long-term design of private insurance plans, making suitable product adjustments, while adding that some employers may opt for co-payments for certain options.
He also said that promoting employee health education and encouraging staff to use network doctors could also ease premium pressure, as network services tend to offer more transparent pricing and better cost control.
Professor Peter Yuen, Dean of the College of Professional and Continuing Education at the Hong Kong Polytechnic University, said greater pricing transparency in private medical services was essential to regulating rising costs.
He added that the government should promote case‑mix reimbursements or package deal schemes to make expenditure on specific diagnoses more predictable, rather than relying on the widely used fee‑for‑service model that could drive up healthcare costs.
In June last year, the government announced plans to introduce draft regulations requiring private hospitals to disclose pricing information for basic service items and around 500 surgical procedures, a move intended to prevent opaque pricing from adding to the strain on the public healthcare system.

data.title: China and Canada at ‘new starting point’ as Mark Carney breaks ice with state visit
data.description:
China and Canada are at a “new starting point” and the two countries should deepen cooperation to protect multilateralism and free trade.
That was the message Premier Li Qiang had for Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney in Beijing on Thursday, on the second day of a four-day state visit to China.
“Standing at a new starting point, China is willing to work with Canada to uphold the strategic partnership, strengthen dialogue and communication, enhance political mutual trust, respect each other’s core interests, seek common ground while shelving differences, and continuously expand pragmatic cooperation to add more impetus to the development of both countries,” he said, according to state broadcaster CCTV.
Li said both countries had worked to achieve a “turnaround” in relations and China would seek to restore and advance exchanges with Canada as well as promote “stable growth in bilateral trade”, calling for cooperation in areas ranging from clean energy to digital technology to agriculture and aerospace.
China, Li added, was willing to deepen cooperation with Canada in international frameworks such as the United Nations and “jointly uphold multilateralism and free trade, promote the improvement of global governance, and push the international order towards a more just and equitable direction”.


Carney, who took office in March last year, is the first Canadian prime minister to visit China since 2017 and is looking to recalibrate ties after years of tension following the arrests in 2018 of a senior official at Chinese telecommunications firm Huawei Technologies and two Canadian citizens.
Carney is also seeking to diversify Canada’s economic relations and reduce the country’s heavy reliance on the US market.
Canada’s steel, aluminium and car sectors have been hit hard by US President Donald Trump’s tariffs, with talks between Ottawa and Washington to ease those duties suspended.
Most Canadian goods can now enter the US thanks to a free-trade agreement between the US, Canada and Mexico. But the pact is up for a review this year, and its extension is uncertain. Trump has repeatedly suggested that Canada, a long-time American ally, could become a US state.
This has prompted Ottawa to reassess the vulnerabilities inherent in its heavy economic reliance on America and set a target of doubling its non-US exports over the next decade.
In addition, Ottawa is seeking to persuade Beijing to remove anti-dumping duties on its canola oil.
Canadian media reported that Carney witnessed the signing of agreements on clean and conventional energy, but there were no deals reached on tariffs.
According to the CCTV report, the Canadian prime minister said Ottawa was willing to deepen dialogue with Beijing and push for cooperation in areas such as trade, energy and people-to-people exchanges.
Carney also met with Zhao Leji, the head of the national legislature, on Thursday and is set to hold talks with Chinese President Xi Jinping on Friday.
A Canadian statement said the two leaders welcomed a series of agreements, including on energy and culture, that would “provide the foundation for a new strategic partnership”.
The statement added that Carney highlighted opportunities in enhanced trade and investment between the two countries and the potential for greater cooperation in energy and agriculture.


When he announced his China trip, Carney said he sought to transform Canada’s economy from one that was reliant on a single trade partner. He also said he wanted to build a more competitive, sustainable and independent economy.
“China is our second-largest trading partner, and the world’s second-largest economy,” Carney said in a social media post on Wednesday.
“A pragmatic and constructive relationship between our nations will create greater stability, security and prosperity on both sides of the Pacific [Ocean].”
China is Canada’s second-largest single-country trading partner, trailing only the United States. Two-way merchandise trade totalled C$118.7 billion (US$85.5 billion) in 2024.
Meanwhile, Chinese customs data released on Wednesday revealed a sharp 10.4 per cent fall in imports from Canada last year to US$41.7 billion, down from a record high of US$46.56 billion a year earlier.
On Thursday, before the Li-Carney meeting, Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi told his Canadian counterpart, Anita Anand, that Beijing would improve communication with Ottawa, eliminate interference and deepen cooperation.
“The world today is undergoing profound and complex changes unprecedented in history, and the impact of China-Canada relations extends beyond the bilateral scope,” Wang was quoted as saying in a Chinese statement.

Anand said Ottawa hoped to define the future path of bilateral ties and restart dialogue across various sectors through the Canadian leader’s visit.
Before the trip, analysts expected trade, energy cooperation and climate change to figure prominently in Carney’s agenda in China.
In particular, Ottawa seeks to persuade Beijing to remove anti-dumping duties on its canola oil, though expectations for an immediate breakthrough are modest.
According to Kpler, an energy data analytics firm, China last year surpassed the US as the top buyer of crude transported via Canada’s Trans Mountain Expansion Project – a year after the expanded pipeline went into operation.
For China, the enlarged pipeline serves a strategic purpose: diversifying its energy portfolio with an open route to buy stable and affordable crude oil from Canada.
At the same time, trade has proved a complex battleground between China and Canada, with Ottawa having aligned closely with Washington.
In 2024, under former Canadian prime minister Justin Trudeau, Ottawa imposed 100 per cent tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles and 25 per cent duties on steel and aluminium.
China hit back with retaliatory tariffs, including a 100 per cent duty on canola oil and a 25 per cent duty on Canadian seafood and pork last March, followed by a levy of almost 76 per cent on canola seed in August.
Anand on Wednesday said talks on the canola issue had been productive and would continue.
“We will be exploring several opportunities for collaboration between the broader populations, in addition to examining the trade and economic relationship,” the Canadian foreign minister added.

data.title: Kremlin warns Ukraine running out of time to accept peace terms as winter crisis deepens
data.description:
The Kremlin said on Thursday that Ukraine was running out of options to strike a deal to end almost four years of war with Russia, as thousands were without heating in Kyiv in -12 degrees Celsius (10 degrees Fahrenheit) temperatures following waves of Russian strikes on the country’s energy grid.
Moscow has not backed down from its intention to seize the rest of east Ukraine as the fourth anniversary of its offensive – Europe’s worst war since World War II – looms.
“The situation is deteriorating day by day for the Kyiv regime,” Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters, adding that Ukraine’s “corridor for decision-making” was “narrowing”.
Russia has rejected Western proposals to end the war and said it is intent on taking the rest of Ukrainian land it has proclaimed as Russian by force if diplomacy fails.
“The time has come for [Ukrainian leader Volodymyr] Zelensky to take responsibility and make the appropriate decision,” Peskov said, apparently urging Kyiv to agree to Moscow’s hardline terms.
Russian President Vladimir Putin has demanded Kyiv withdraw from swathes of eastern and southern Ukraine as a precondition to a ceasefire.

US President Donald Trump has expressed frustration with both sides, with no breakthrough to end the war reached after months of talks.
Ukrainian, US and European negotiators have agreed a framework 20-point plan to end the war, Zelensky said – an adjustment to a 28-point proposal initially tabled by Washington that heavily favoured Moscow.
The Kremlin has balked at any adjustments.
Peskov said that dialogue was continuing with the United States.

The Europeans had many talks with the Americans, he said, and that “it is important that the Russian side also present its opinions on these discussions”.
Moscow had rejected a declaration that Britain and France would send troops to Ukraine after a ceasefire with Russia, calling them potential “legitimate targets” for Moscow.
February 24 will mark four years since the Kremlin launched its full-scale offensive on Ukraine.
Tens of thousands have been killed, much of eastern Ukraine has been decimated and millions have been forced to flee their homes.
